title: Echinopsis puquiensis
taxonomy: Echinopsis puquiensis is classified as a species within the genus Echinopsis, which belongs to the family Cactaceae and the order Caryophyllales. The family Cactaceae is a group of flowering plants commonly known as cacti, characterized by their unique, often spiny stems and adaptations for water conservation. Echinopsis puquiensis is a member of this diverse group, which comprises over 180 genera and 1,500 species of cacti. The genus Echinopsis is further divided into several sections, with Echinopsis puquiensis being one of the species within section Echinopsis. This classification reflects the plant's evolutionary relationships and shared characteristics with other members of the Echinopsis and Cactaceae lineages.

intro: Echinopsis puquiensis, also known as Queñua, is a type of cactus that belongs to the Echinopsis genus, which is native to the Andean highlands of South America. The plant is rich in bioactive constituents, including alkaloids, flavonoids, and saponins, which have been found to possess antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties, contributing to its potential health benefits such as wound healing, immune system support, and protection against oxidative stress. The bioactive constituents of Echinopsis puquiensis, particularly the alkaloid puquioside, have been identified as the key compounds responsible for its medicinal properties. Traditionally, Echinopsis puquiensis is used to prepare various herbal preparations, including teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat a range of health conditions, including skin problems, respiratory issues, and digestive disorders.
